title: TPU v1 · Clock-domain-crossing RTL: safely transferring signals among the PCIe, core-array, and DDR3 domains
oneLine: The host issues commands through PCIe, the on-chip 256×256 array runs at 700 MHz, and off-chip DDR3 operates in another clock domain. A signal crossing these domains without protection can become metastable. This case covers the standard CDC structures—two-flop synchronizers for single-bit controls and asynchronous FIFOs or handshakes for multibit payloads—and explains why CDC defects often pass simulation yet fail intermittently in silicon.

legend.name: Source-domain register (sender)
legend.desc: A signal registered in the source clock domain, such as clk_pcie. Its transition is asynchronous to the destination clock and may land inside the setup/hold aperture of a destination flip-flop—the root cause of CDC risk.

legend.name: Two-flop synchronizer (single bit)
legend.desc: Two cascaded flip-flops clocked by the destination domain (clk_core). The first stage absorbs possible metastability and receives a full cycle to resolve; the second stage is overwhelmingly likely to sample a stable value before it enters core logic. This is the standard minimum structure for a single-bit CDC.

legend.name: Metastability (direct-crossing failure)
legend.desc: If an asynchronous signal directly drives destination logic, a flip-flop can remain between 0 and 1 for an unpredictable interval. Reconvergent paths may then observe inconsistent values and corrupt behavior. Ideal 0/1 functional simulation cannot expose this, so the design may pass simulation yet fail randomly after fabrication.

legend.name: Asynchronous FIFO / handshake (multibit)
legend.desc: A multibit bus must never be synchronized bit by bit; unequal arrival times can create a word that never existed at the source. Instead, store data in dual-port RAM and transfer Gray-coded read/write pointers—where only one bit changes at a time—through two-flop synchronizers so the payload crosses atomically and remains aligned.

legend.name: CDC lint (static analysis)
legend.desc: Because ordinary functional simulation cannot reveal metastability, dedicated CDC analysis must enumerate every crossing and confirm it passes through a recognized synchronizer or asynchronous FIFO, with no unprotected signal crossing a domain boundary.

steps.cap: ① A source register in the PCIe domain launches a single-bit control toward the core domain. The transition is asynchronous to clk_core and can occur inside a core flip-flop's sampling aperture.

② At the core-domain boundary, pass the signal through a two-flop synchronizer. The first stage may briefly become metastable and receives one cycle to resolve; the second stage is overwhelmingly likely to deliver a clean 0 or 1 to the array logic.

③ Counterexample: a direct asynchronous connection can drive a flip-flop metastable, leaving its output between 0 and 1 and allowing downstream paths to sample inconsistent values. Functional simulation misses this; silicon can fail intermittently.

④ For a multibit transfer such as a host command or address, do not synchronize bits independently. Use an asynchronous FIFO or bundled-data handshake: data enters dual-port RAM and synchronized Gray-coded pointers preserve whole-word alignment across domains.

⑤ Run CDC lint after implementation. It statically checks every crossing for a recognized synchronizer or FIFO and flags all unprotected paths—an essential structural signoff that functional simulation cannot replace.

before: Inventory clock domains and draw their boundaries: TPU v1 necessarily spans at least the host PCIe interface, the 700 MHz on-chip core array, and the off-chip DDR3 interface. Identify every asynchronous clock and every signal that crosses between them; each crossing is a potential metastability site.

Classify each crossing as single-bit or multibit: use a two-flop synchronizer for a single control such as valid, ready, or enable. Use an asynchronous FIFO or a bundled-data handshake for an address, data word, or instruction. This classification determines the RTL structure and must be settled before coding.

Standardize reset and synchronizer style: define the synchronizer depth (typically two stages, or three when extra MTBF margin is required), reset policy, and naming convention such as *_sync2 so CDC tools can recognize the structure consistently.

Plan for a problem simulation cannot cover: metastability does not exist in ideal two-state/logic-level functional simulation, and gate simulation models it only partially with specialized timing or statistical models. Make CDC static analysis a mandatory acceptance gate from the beginning.
during: For one bit, use a proper two-stage synchronizer: in the destination domain, code two cascaded registers clocked by clk_core—for example, reg a_sync1, a_sync2; always_ff @(posedge clk_core) begin a_sync1 <= a_async; a_sync2 <= a_sync1; end. Downstream logic may use only a_sync2; a_sync1 is the metastability-absorbing stage.

Never synchronize a multibit bus bit by bit: separate synchronizers can resolve on different cycles and produce a torn word that the source never sent. Multibit payloads require an asynchronous FIFO or a bundled-data protocol in which data is held stable and a synchronized control qualifies the entire word.

Gray-coded pointers are central to an asynchronous FIFO: read and write pointers must cross domains to compute empty/full. Gray coding changes only one pointer bit per increment; when sampled near a transition, the destination sees either the old or new code rather than a hazardous multibit intermediate combination.

Trade latency for safety: synchronizers inherently add one or two cycles, and FIFOs add more. That latency is the cost of a safe crossing. “The signal changes slowly, so a direct connection should be fine” is not a valid exception; every CDC must use an appropriate synchronizer or FIFO.

Preserve synchronizer structure through synthesis: synthesis may otherwise merge or optimize the stages and reduce metastability resolution time. Encapsulate synchronizers in dedicated cells or use appropriate preservation attributes, and constrain asynchronous crossings with false-path/max-delay policies so ordinary timing closure does not distort the CDC structure.

Scope note: TPU-specific CDC details are not public: the PCIe, core, and DDR3 domains follow from TPU v1's documented architecture, but synchronizer depth, FIFO depth, and handshake protocol are implementation details. The structures here are standard multi-clock ASIC practice, not claimed TPU-specific RTL.

result.label: Single-bit CDC
result.value: Two-flop synchronizer clocked by the destination domain

result.label: Multibit CDC
result.value: Asynchronous FIFO / bundled-data handshake; never per-bit synchronization

result.label: FIFO pointer encoding
result.value: Gray code (one bit changes per step), then synchronized

result.label: Clock domains involved
result.value: PCIe domain · 700 MHz core-array domain · DDR3 memory domain

result.label: Primary signoff method
result.value: Static CDC analysis (ordinary simulation is insufficient)

after: Run dedicated CDC static analysis: enumerate every clock-domain crossing and verify that each uses a recognized synchronizer or asynchronous FIFO. Also detect shared synchronizers, reconvergent paths, and multibit payloads incorrectly treated as independent single-bit crossings.

Use metastability-injection simulation: inject random delay or metastability models at crossing registers so simulation can exercise downstream robustness. Passing ideal functional simulation alone is not evidence of CDC safety.

Inspect constraints and synthesis attributes: confirm crossings have the intended false-path/max-delay constraints, synchronizer cells retain their preservation attributes, and both stages remain intact after synthesis.

Review multi-clock operating scenarios with verification: cover reset sequencing, clock start/stop behavior, and cross-domain valid/ready handshakes. CDC defects are high-risk because they can surface only intermittently in silicon, so structure, tools, and human review must all sign off at RTL.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017 (host commands over PCIe, a 700 MHz core array, and off-chip DDR3 establish the documented multi-clock context; two-flop synchronizers, asynchronous FIFOs, and CDC analysis are standard RTL practices, while TPU's detailed CDC implementation is not public)
